records.display_name: Fordham Road declining bus speeds

payload.claim_text: Significant and consistent decline in bus speeds in recent years on Fordham Road. High curb demand, frequently blocked bus lanes, traffic and sidewalk congestion.

records.display_name: Washington Bridge bus lane benefits

payload.claim_text: Proposed bus lanes on Washington Bridge would provide direct decrease in travel times for 36,800 bus riders and improved reliability for 111,400 riders on all 5 MTA bus routes, as well as improved bus speeds for all other buses including school buses and non-MTA buses.

records.display_name: Better Buses Action Plan goals

payload.claim_text: Mayor's 2019 State of the City: Improve bus speeds 25% by 2020. Better Buses Action Plan released April 2019. 24 priority projects announced for 2019 to improve bus service across all 5 boroughs.

records.display_name: Bronx corridor selection methodology

payload.claim_text: As part of MTA's Bronx Bus Network Redesign process, NYC DOT analyzed 46 major Bronx corridors to identify where bus lanes and other treatments would speed up buses and allow the MTA to operate more frequent service. NYC DOT selected 10 of the highest ranking corridors, including 4 within CB5: Tremont Avenue, University Avenue, Washington Bridge / 181 St, Fordham Road / 207 St / University Heights Bridge.

records.display_name: University Avenue Corridor

description: Bus corridor serving Bx3, Bx36. Combined 55,000 weekday riders, 12,700 weekday riders on University Ave. Bus speeds 6.3 mph during AM Peak, 5.6 mph during PM Peak. Key issues: slow bus speeds and traffic congestion, buses frequently cannot pull to curb, double parking, long distances between pedestrian crossings.
SBS candidate corridor identified for next phase of SBS program

records.display_name: Better Buses Action Plan

description: The Better Buses Action Plan is focused on improving bus speeds citywide by 25% and reversing downward bus ridership trends. Accomplished through NYC DOT projects using the bus priority toolkit, increased camera and NYPD enforcement of bus lanes, and service management initiatives and bus network redesigns by MTA.
Set goals of increasing bus speeds by 25% citywide, installing 10-15 miles of new bus lanes per year, installing TSP at hundreds of intersections, and piloting new busways and automated enforcement
2018 NYC Department of Transportation plan that established a performance target of increasing bus speeds by 25% citywide—from approximately 8 to 10 miles per hour. Target has since been abandoned.
The source identifies the 2018 NYC DOT Better Buses plan's citywide 25 percent bus-speed target and says that target has since been abandoned.

de Blasio Administration's 2019 plan to improve bus service across the City, focused on increasing citywide bus speeds by 25% and reversing downward ridership trends, establishing goals such as improving 5 miles of existing bus lanes each year, installing 10-15 miles of new bus lanes each year, expanding bus lane camera enforcement, and advocating with the MTA for all-door boarding.

Mayor's 2019 State of the City initiative to improve bus speeds 25% by 2020. Better Buses Action Plan released April 2019. 24 priority projects announced for 2019 to increase bus speeds across all 5 boroughs.
Mayor's 2019 State of the City. Improve bus speeds 25% by 2020. Better Buses Action Plan released April 2019. 24 priority projects announced for 2019 to increase bus speeds across all 5 boroughs
Mayor's 2019 State of the City initiative to improve bus speeds 25% by 2020. 24 priority projects announced for 2019 across all 5 boroughs.

Mayor's 2019 State of the City: Improve bus speeds 25%. Released April 2019. Included 16.3 miles of bus lanes installed in 2020 and 8 additional corridors including Fordham Rd identified in the Bronx for priority treatments.

NYC DOT analyzed 46 major Bronx corridors as part of MTA's Bronx Bus Network Redesign process. Selected 10 highest ranking corridors, including 4 within CB5: Tremont Avenue, University Avenue, Washington Bridge/181 St, Fordham Road/207 St/University Heights Bridge. Study of potential treatments including bus lanes, queue jump signals, bus boarding islands, short segments of bus/bike lanes, offset bus lanes.

Mayor's 2019 State of the City: Improve bus speeds 25%. Released April 2019. Included 16.3 miles of bus lanes installed in 2020, including projects on Edward L Grant Hwy & E 149 St. Identified 8 additional corridors including Fordham Rd.
Mayor's 2019 State of the City: improve bus speeds 25% by 2020; Better Buses Action Plan released April 2019; 24 priority projects announced for 2019
Mayor's 2019 State of the City initiative to improve bus speeds 25% by 2020; released April 2019; 24 priority projects announced for 2019 across all 5 boroughs

records.display_name: 2020 Bus Priority Improvements – Bronx Community Board 5

payload.description: Presentation to Bronx Community Board 5 Municipal Services Committee on December 3, 2019, covering 2020 bus priority improvements including corridors Tremont Avenue, University Avenue, Washington Bridge/181 St, and Fordham Road/207 St/University Heights Bridge.
payload.title: 2020 Bus Priority Improvements

records.display_name: University Avenue and Fordham Rd queue jump signal

payload.description: Investigating queue jump signal at this intersection in both directions. Potential treatment shows 12' queue jump/right turn bay, 5' bike lane. Benefits: buses and bikes can bypass general traffic, increased bus speeds, reduced vehicle/bus/bike conflicts.

records.display_name: Washington Bridge bus lanes proposal

payload.description: Proposed bus lanes on Washington Bridge. Potential treatment: converting two 10.5' travel lanes to 10.5' bus lanes (one curb lane in each direction). Benefits: increased bus speeds, direct decrease in travel times for 36,800 bus riders, improved reliability for 111,400 riders on all 5 MTA bus routes.
payload.location_text: Washington Bridge, Amsterdam Ave to University Ave

normalized_location.raw_text: Washington Bridge, Amsterdam Ave to University Ave

records.display_name: University Avenue south of Tremont Ave treatment study

payload.description: Potential treatment: 10.5' bus lane, 8.5' boarding island. Benefits: increased bus speeds by eliminating weaving in/out of stops, improved bus stop accessibility, reduced crossing distances and improved pedestrian safety, enhanced bicycle safety and comfort, reduced sidewalk congestion.
payload.location_text: University Avenue, South of Tremont Ave

normalized_location.raw_text: University Avenue, South of Tremont Ave

records.display_name: University Avenue north of Tremont Ave treatment study

payload.description: Potential treatment: 11' shared bike/bus lane, 9' boarding island. Benefits: streamlined boarding, increased bus speeds by eliminating weaving in/out of stops, improved bus stop accessibility, reduced vehicle/bicycle conflicts, reduced sidewalk congestion.
payload.location_text: University Avenue, North of Tremont Ave

normalized_location.raw_text: University Avenue, North of Tremont Ave

records.display_name: Tremont Ave b/n Anthony Ave and Valentine Ave treatment study

payload.description: Potential treatment showing new 11' bus lane replacing existing 11' shared lane. Benefits: increased bus speeds by eliminating weaving in/out of traffic.
payload.location_text: Tremont Avenue between Anthony Ave and Valentine Ave

normalized_location.raw_text: Tremont Avenue between Anthony Ave and Valentine Ave

records.display_name: Tremont Ave b/n Morris Ave & Grand Concourse treatment study

payload.description: Potential treatment studying elimination of curbside parking lane to create dedicated bus stop area. Benefits: increased bus speeds by eliminating weaving in/out of traffic, improved bus stop accessibility. Potential treatment shows 10' travel lane and 13' bus stop replacing 14' shared lane and 9' parking lane.
payload.location_text: Tremont Ave between Morris Ave and Grand Concourse

normalized_location.raw_text: Tremont Ave between Morris Ave and Grand Concourse
